source_paper_title: Satiation in Fisher Markets and Approximation of Nash Social Welfare
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Garg_2024, title={Satiation in Fisher Markets and Approximation of Nash Social Welfare}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2019.0129}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2019.0129}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Garg, Jugal and Hoefer, Martin and Mehlhorn, Kurt}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={1109–1139} }
context:
Section 5 (Future Directions), page 32.

Beyond money-clearing markets, however, even deciding existence is not well-understood – is it NP-hard, or can it be tightly characterized by a simple condition that can be checked in polynomial time? In addition to deciding existence, can we efficiently find exact equilibria in general (if they exist) or in money-clearing markets?
title: Deciding existence of equilibria in Fisher markets with earning and utility limits
background_latex:
A (linear) Fisher market with satiation consists of:
1) A set of buyers \(B\) and a set of divisible goods \(G\), each good \(j\in G\) having unit supply.
2) For each buyer \(i\in B\): a budget \(m_i\ge 0\), a utility cap \(c_i>0\), and per-unit values \(u_{ij}\ge 0\) for goods \(j\in G\).
3) For each good \(j\in G\): an earning (income) cap \(d_j>0\).

An allocation is a matrix \(x=(x_{ij})_{i\in B, j\in G}\) with \(x_{ij}\in[0,1]\) and \(\sum_{i\in B} x_{ij}=x_j\le 1\) for each good \(j\). Buyer \(i\)'s budget-additive utility is
\[
U_i(x_i)=\min\Bigl(c_i,\;\sum_{j\in G} u_{ij}x_{ij}\Bigr),\qquad x_i=(x_{ij})_{j\in G}.
\]
Let \(p=(p_j)_{j\in G}\) be nonnegative prices. Given prices \(p\), define buyer \(i\)'s maximum bang-per-buck (MBB) ratio as
\[
\alpha_i=\max_{j\in G: p_j>0}\frac{u_{ij}}{p_j},
\]
(with the convention that goods with \(p_j=0\) are treated separately). A bundle is MBB (also called thrifty) for buyer \(i\) if it assigns positive amount only to goods attaining the maximum ratio \(u_{ij}/p_j=\alpha_i\).

A demand bundle for buyer \(i\) at prices \(p\) is any \(x_i\) that maximizes \(U_i(x_i)\) subject to the budget constraint \(\sum_j p_j x_{ij}\le m_i\). A demand bundle is called modest if it does not overshoot the cap, i.e., \(\sum_j u_{ij}x_{ij}\le c_i\) (equivalently, if capped then it hits \(c_i\) exactly).

For each good \(j\), a seller chooses an effective supply \(e_j\le 1\) to maximize revenue up to its earning cap, i.e., earns \(\min(d_j, p_j e_j)\). A modest supply at prices \(p\) is
\[
e_j=\min\Bigl(1,\frac{d_j}{p_j}\Bigr)
\]
(with \(e_j=1\) if \(p_j=0\) by continuity).

A thrifty and modest equilibrium is a pair \((x,p)\) such that:
1) \(p_j\ge 0\) for all \(j\).
2) Each buyer \(i\) receives a thrifty (MBB) and modest demand bundle at prices \(p\).
3) Each good \(j\) uses a modest supply \(e_j\) and is not overallocated: \(x_j\le e_j\).
4) Walras' law holds: \(p_j(e_j-x_j)=0\) for all \(j\) (so if a good is not fully sold under its modest supply, its price is zero).

The computational decision problem asks, given integer input data \((u_{ij},m_i,c_i,d_j)\), whether at least one thrifty and modest equilibrium exists.
problem_definition_latex:
Given a Fisher market instance specified by \(B,G\) and nonnegative parameters \((u_{ij})\), \((m_i)\), \((c_i)\), and \((d_j)\), determine whether there exists a thrifty and modest equilibrium \((x,p)\) satisfying:
\[
\text{(i) } p\ge 0;\qquad \text{(ii) each }x_i\text{ is an MBB and modest demand bundle at }p;\qquad \text{(iii) }x_j\le \min\Bigl(1,\frac{d_j}{p_j}\Bigr);\qquad \text{(iv) } p_j\Bigl(\min\Bigl(1,\frac{d_j}{p_j}\Bigr)-x_j\Bigr)=0\ \forall j.
\]
Equivalently, decide the existence (and, if desired, compute one) of such an equilibrium for markets with both earning caps \((d_j)\) and utility caps \((c_i)\).

\[

\]
(with \(e_j=1\) if \(p_j=0\) by continuity).

A thrifty and modest equilibrium is a pair \((x,p)\) such that:

Given a Fisher market instance specified by \(B,G\) and nonnegative parameters \((u_{ij})\), \((m_i)\), \((c_i)\), and \((d_j)\), determine whether there exists a thrifty and modest equilibrium \((x,p)\) satisfying:
\[
\text{(i) } p\ge 0;\qquad \text{(ii) each }x_i\text{ is an MBB and modest demand bundle at }p;\qquad \text{(iii) }x_j\le \min\Bigl(1,\frac{d_j}{p_j}\Bigr);\qquad \text{(iv) } p_j\Bigl(\min\Bigl(1,\frac{d_j}{p_j}\Bigr)-x_j\Bigr)=0\ \forall j.
\]
Equivalently, decide the existence (and, if desired, compute one) of such an equilibrium for markets with both earning caps \((d_j)\) and utility caps \((c_i)\).
open_problem_quote: Beyond money-clearing markets, however, even deciding existence is not well-understood – is it NP-hard, or can it be tightly characterized by a simple condition that can be checked in polynomial time? In addition to deciding existence, can we efficiently find exact equilibria in general (if they exist) or in money-clearing markets?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is primarily about worst-case complexity (NP-hardness vs. a polynomial-time characterization), not about refuting a specific conjectured inequality or bound on finite instances. While one can search for small instances lacking equilibria, that does not resolve the complexity-theoretic alternatives posed.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could help by automatically generating and testing small market instances for equilibrium existence (e.g., via MILP/LCP feasibility checks), potentially suggesting structural characterizations or hardness gadgets. However, turning such experimental evidence into an NP-hardness proof or a provably correct polynomial-time condition likely requires substantial human-guided theory beyond what an evolutionary search can directly optimize.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
